title: "Emerald Requiem Problem v49.13"

  description: "At 22:05, the Emerald Requiem vanished from a locked display in the southern archives. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had verified clearance near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements, while the other three speak only truths. Only one suspect is truly guilty. Each suspect provides four statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I left this archive at 21:55, so I am not the one who took it at 22:05.'
      (2) 'If 2 is telling the truth, then 3 must have approached the display at 22:00.'
      (3) '4 was nowhere near the display after 21:50.'
      (4) 'Either 2 never entered the archives after 21:45, or 3 is lying — but not both.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 stayed until 22:10, contradicting that 21:55 claim.'
      (2) 'If 3 is lying, then 4 must also be lying.'
      (3) 'I was not near the Emerald Requiem after 21:40, so I cannot be guilty.'
      (4) 'Either 1’s departure time is false, or 3 is guilty, but never both.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I never touched the display after 21:35.'
      (2) 'If 4 is truthful, then 2 cannot be the culprit.'
      (3) 'Either 2 is lying, or 1 lied about leaving early — but not both.'
      (4) 'I am not the thief; logs place me in another corridor at 21:55.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 definitely was near the display at 21:50, contradicting 3’s denial.'
      (2) 'If 1 is truthful, 2 must be guilty.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; logs show me in a different wing at 22:00.'
      (4) 'Either 2 was around the display after 21:40, or 1’s 21:55 is incorrect — not both.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Emerald Requiem? Return one digit only."

answer: >
  explanation: "Predicate Logic Approach:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all statements,' T(x) = 'x tells truth in all statements,' G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one suspect L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true, others T(x)=true.

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => 1 didn't leave 21:55 => possibly present at 22:05 => suspicious
      (2) false => if 2 is truth => 3 approached display 22:00 is not correct => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 4 was near display after 21:50 => might be suspicious
      (4) false => 'Either 2 never entered after 21:45 or 3 is lying not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => 2(1) '1 stayed 22:10' consistent with 1's (1) false. Checking blame leads to contradictions with 3(2) or 4(1). Typically fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3),(4) false =>
      (1) false => 1 did not stay 22:10 => maybe left 21:55
      (2) false => if 3 is lying => 4 is lying is undone => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 2 was near Emerald after 21:40 => suspicious
      (4) false => 'Either 1’s departure time is false or 3 is guilty not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,3,4 truthful => 1(1) 'Left 21:55 => not thief' => G(1)=false, 3(4) 'I am not thief => logs corridor' => G(3)=false, 4(3) 'I am not guilty => G(4)=false => G(2)=true with no contradiction.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3),(4) false => 
      (1) false => 3 did handle display after 21:35 => potential guilt
      (2) false => if 4 is truth => 2 not culprit is undone => 2 could be culprit
      (3) false => 'Either 2 lying or 1 lied about leaving early not both' undone => might be both or neither
      (4) false => 3 might be culprit or logs are false. Meanwhile 1,2,4 are truthful => times or blame conflicts. Typically fails.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2),(3),(4) false => 
      (1) false => 3 wasn't near 21:50 => consistent with 3’s denial if 3 is T
      (2) false => if 1 is truth => 2 must be guilty undone => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 4 is guilty or logs are untrue
      (4) false => 'Either 2 around display or 1’s 21:55 is incorrect not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,2,3 truthful => 1(1) 'Left 21:55 => not guilty' => G(1)=false, 2(3) 'not near after 21:40 => not guilty' => G(2)=false, 3(4) 'I am not culprit => G(3)=false => G(4)=true. No contradiction.

  So L(2)=>G(2) or L(4)=>G(4) can appear. Check 3(2) 'If 4 is truthful => 2 not culprit' if 4 is truthful => G(2)=false => but we want G(2)=true is contradiction => so L(2)=>G(2) fails. Then L(4)=>G(4) stands. The culprit is suspect 4.

  final: '4'"
